text: Welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery. Today, we're talking about the repair of a large spagalian hernia. This was a really interesting case. A sixty-year-old man was presented with a chronic bulge for many years. He had increased abdominal pain around the bulge and his general practitioner got a CT scan.
The cross-sectional imaging reveals a spigelian hernia dissecting between the external and internal oblique muscles, along with bilateral fat-containing direct hernias.

The video shows a large Spigelian hernia defect measuring approximately six by five centimeters, dissecting the left abdominal wall and containing the colon, small bowel, and omentum. The surgical plan involves a contralateral docking approach, working from the patient's right to left.
The laparoscopic camera view shows the manual reduction of most incarcerated contents, leaving only the descending colon drawn up with the retroperitoneum. A robust preperitoneal fat pad is visible.

The surgeon begins preperitoneal dissection, circumferentially dissecting around the hernia defect at the neck of the hernia sac. The dissection progresses towards the lateral abdominal wall, removing areolar tissue.

The surgeon approaches the medial margin of the hernia, with the rectus abdominis muscle visible. The hernia sac reduction is performed at the inferior and medial aspects of the hernia using two robotic arms.
The surgeon reduces a large hernia sac, which is visually straightforward due to the lack of chronic adhesions, resulting in a satisfying reduction.

The surgeon identifies the lateral margin of the hernia and invaginates the hernia sac to prevent it from falling back into the space. Cephalad dissection is performed before completing the reduction of the hernia sac. The surgeon then works on circumferential dissection, including the dissection of the myopectineal orifice on the patient's left side, while visualizing the spermatic vessels and the direct hernia in the retropubic space.
The surgeon reduces the left direct hernia, providing a clear view of the Spigelian defect. The dissection around the myopectineal orifice is completed, ensuring good coverage of the defect.

The surgeon prepares for defect closure and mesh placement, using an 18-inch old permanent V-Lock suture on a GS-XXII needle. A port is introduced cephalad, initially intended for large heavyweight mesh placement, but overlapping mid-weight meshes are chosen instead, making the trocar placement likely unnecessary.

The surgeon places trocars strategically to ensure they will be covered by the mesh, preventing trocar site hernias. Sequential tightening and traction are applied to the defect for closure. The insufflation is reduced to about ten to facilitate easy closure without tension.

The surgeon uses an eighteen-inch V-lock suture to close the defect, followed by the placement and fixation of a mid-weight extra-large mesh over the groin area. The retromuscular and preperitoneal spaces are covered with a barred soft mesh, ensuring broad coverage of at least ten centimeters in all directions, extending to the midline.

The surgeon places multiple interrupted stitches to secure the mesh over the hernia site, applying fibrin sealant for additional fixation. The flap closure is performed near the hernia sac, which presents challenges due to proximity to the surgical site. An eighteen-inch suture is used, though it is noted to be excessively long for the task.

The surgeon confirms that the mesh is completely excluded from the viscera. The trocar used for mesh introduction is retracted to release preperitoneal air, and a drain is passed into the hernia space through the same trocar.
